coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots/ZSM-5 type metalosilicate composites and evaluating their performance in photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue and electrochemical water splitting

coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/ZSM-5 type cobaltosilicate and GQDs/ZSM-5 type chromosilicate composites were prepared by two methods; direct and solid state dispersion (SSD) method. The composites were characterized using XRD, FT-IR, UV–vis absorption, and SEM techniques. The photocatalytic activities of composites were evaluated by degradation of methylene blue (MB) under both UV and vis light irradiation. The composites prepared by direct method showed better photocatalytic activity and the GQDs/chromosilicate composite had a superior performance. This composite could remove about 96 % and 87 % of MB at first 30 min under UV and vis light, respectively. The results of photocatalytic degradation kinetic studies revealed that the composites follow the pseudo-second-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.99) with rate constants ranging from 0.295 to 0.581 g mg−1 min−1 for different composites prepared by direct method. The GQDs/metalosilicate composites were also examined for the electrochemical water splitting. The GQDs/cobaltosilicate composites required lower overpotentials for HER and OER in comparison to the pure GDQs and cobaltosilicate. Moreover, the charge transfer resistance and Warburg impedance was lower than the corresponding values obtained for the pure cobaltosilicate and GQDs. These new GQDs-based composites are expected to open new windows in materials science and electrocatalytic-related processes.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots/ZSM-5 type metalosilicate composites and evaluating their performance in photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue and electrochemical water splitting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/ZSM-5 type cobaltosilicate and GQDs/ZSM-5 type chromosilicate composites were prepared by two methods; direct and solid state dispersion (SSD) method. The composites were characterized using XRD, FT-IR, UV–vis absorption, and SEM techniques. The photocatalytic activities of composites were evaluated by degradation of methylene blue (MB) under both UV and vis light irradiation. The composites prepared by direct method showed better photocatalytic activity and the GQDs/chromosilicate composite had a superior performance. This composite could remove about 96 % and 87 % of MB at first 30 min under UV and vis light, respectively. The results of photocatalytic degradation kinetic studies revealed that the composites follow the pseudo-second-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.99) with rate constants ranging from 0.295 to 0.581 g mg−1 min−1 for different composites prepared by direct method. The GQDs/metalosilicate composites were also examined for the electrochemical water splitting. The GQDs/cobaltosilicate composites required lower overpotentials for HER and OER in comparison to the pure GDQs and cobaltosilicate. Moreover, the charge transfer resistance and Warburg impedance was lower than the corresponding values obtained for the pure cobaltosilicate and GQDs. These new GQDs-based composites are expected to open new windows in materials science and electrocatalytic-related processes.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), developed by Ponomarenko and Geim [1] have received tremendous attention due to their excellent physical and chemical properties. GQDs are zero dimensional carbon nanomaterials with particles smaller than 10 nm that are considered as non-zero band gap semiconductors [2–4]. The band gap energy of GQDs can be manipulated by changing their particle size [5,6]. Because of this property and due to the presence of extended p-electron system, GQDs show unique optical properties which leads to their extensive applications in photovoltaic devices [7], solar cells [8], fluorescent agents [9] and light emitting diodes [10]. GQDs also benefit from high surface area, high solubility, optical stability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and stable luminescence [2,11,12,4]. GQDs show photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation that make it possible to use these nanomaterials for the degradation of organic pollutants [13,14]. They can be considered as alternatives to metal oxide semiconductors with wide band gap that are prepared from expensive precursors and may have side effects on the living organs [15]. Fan et al. have investigated the photocatalytic performance of GQDs and polymer-modified GQDs in the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under visible light [16]. According to the results, GQDs performed as effective visible light photocatalysts in the degradation of organic matter. The results also confirmed that the polymer (polyethylenimine and polyethylene glycol)-modified GQDs qualitatively control the degradation rate of MB. The photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange and methylene blue using GQDs synthesized from pyrocatechol have been studied by Mandal et al. [17]. The degradation efficiency was 52 % and 79 % for methyl orange and methylene blue, respectively. The reaction rate constant was also higher for the degradation of methylene blue.

Zeolites, an important class of porous materials were usually applied as supports for the immobilizing of photocatalysts. It was reported that the presence of inert supports with suitable adsorption properties could enhance the efficiency of degradation [18,19]. The improved adsorption of pollutants by supported photocatalysts results in the higher concentration of pollutants in the vicinity of photocatalyst active sites. So, the subsequent degradation of pollutants can take place on the active sites of photocatalyst more effectively [20,21]. Furthermore, the presence of porous supports provides the uniform dispersion of photocatalysts which enhances the number of active sites and the catalyst surface area [22]. Zeolites as supports can also inhibit the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs because of their electron acceptor/donor property [23]. Zhang et al. have reported the preparation of TiO2/natural zeolite and TiO2/acid leached zeolite composites for the removal of different pollutants including formaldehyde, phenol, and rhodamine B. The TiO2/acid leached zeolite composite exhibited higher degradation efficiency for most of the pollutants which was attributed to the higher surface area of the acid leached zeolite and excellent dispersion of nanoparticles on the zeolite surface [18]. Crystalline zeolite immobilized TiO2 nanoparticles have been prepared using liquid impregnation technique by Saqib et al. [23]. The presence of zeolite as support improved the degradation of methylene blue by the photocatalyst under UV irradiation. ZnO-zeolite nanocomposites have been examined for the degradation of benzophenone 3 and caffeine. According to the results, the best degradation efficiency was obtained for the composite containing 10 % ZnO [24].

Water splitting by electrochemical method has drawn most attention due to the concerns regarding the energy crisis and environment pollution. Hydrogen which is considered as a promising clean fuel for the future can be produced by the water splitting. By now, different catalysts including metal nanoparticles, metal oxides, sulfides, and nitrides, layered double hydroxides (LDHs) and carbon nano materials have been examined as electrocatalysts for the two half-reactions involved in the water splitting; hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [25–28]. There are few studies of using GQDs and its composites as electrocatalysts for water splitting. Guo et al. prepared a coral-shaped monolayer MoS2 with a large amount of exposed edge sites which was decorated with GQDs [29]. The improvement of electrical conductivity by adding GQDs led to the outstanding electrocatalytic performance with small onset overpotential of 95 mV for HER. The prepared two-dimensional HER electrocatalyst showed excellent long-term electrocatalytic stability up to 2000 cycles. Cirone et al. evaluated the performance of cobalt/graphene nanocomposite quantum dots as OER catalyst [30]. The results revealed a very high current density (43.16 mA cm−2) for the nanocomposite, whereas smaller current densities were achieved for Co nanoparticles and Pt/C commercial catalyst. The nanocomposite electrocatalyst also benefited from small overpotential (0.49 V) and high stability. Electrostatic adsorbing GQDs into nickel–based LDHs (NiM-LDHs (M = Fe, Co, Mn)) by Guo et al. resulted in enhanced OER activity [27]. The effective factor on this improved OER activity was the adjustment of the charge distribution by the electron transfer between Ni-based LDHs and GQDs. Considering the previous studies, it seems that GQDs composites can be regarded as promising electrocatalysts for the OER and HER.

This work focuses on the synthesis of GQDs/ZSM-5 type metalosilicate composites and their application as photocatalysts and electrocatalysts. To the best of our knowledge, the synthesis of GQDs/ZSM-5 type metalosilicates has not been reported previously and is studied in this work for the first time. The photocatalytic activity of composites is evaluated by the degradation of methylene blue which is one of the frequently used dyes in different industries. Zeolites are potential candidates for the adsorption of dye molecules and several authors reported the adsorption of MB by zeolites [31,32], so in this work we use zeolite type metalosilicates as supports for GQDs. Moreover the performance of GQDs/metalosilicate composites as electrocatalysts for OER and HER is investigated and the results are discussed.

Silicic acid (SiO2·xH2O), cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), Chromium (III) nitrate nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3·9H2O), tetrapropylammonium bromide (TPABr), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), nitric acid (HNO3), citric acid (CA), and methylene blue were purchased from Merck chemical company and used without further purification.

The GQDs were prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) according to the literature [33]. The method is simple and environment-friendly, and provides water-soluble GQDs while minimizing the number of synthesis steps [16]. Typically, a weighted amount of CA (2 g) was put into a crucible and heated at 200 °C for 30 min. After this period, a light orange product was obtained that confirmed the synthesis of GQDs. By increasing the heating time, the color of the product changes and turns into brownish orange due to the increasing of GQDs particle size. So, the heating time of 30 min was chosen as the best time for the synthesis of GQDs. Then, a solution of NaOH (100 mL, 0.25 mol/L) was added dropwise to the obtained light orange suspension until pH = 7 under vigorous stirring. Finally, the solvent was evaporated and the product was dried at 100 °C for 24 h.

The synthesis procedure of cobaltosilicates is described in our previous work [34]. Accordingly, silicic acid, tetrapropylammonium bromide (TPABr), cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), sodium hydroxide, and distilled water were mixed together to obtain the molar ratios of H2O/Si = 22.66, TPABr/Si = 0.17, Na/Si = 1.5 and Co/Si = 0.02. The pH of the mixture was adjusted to 9.5 or 10.5 for the synthesis of Na[Co]ZSM-59.5 and Na[Co]ZSM-510.5, respectively. The crystallization of samples was carried out in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 160 °C for 120 h. The products were washed with distilled water several times, dried in an oven at 110 °C overnight and calcined in air at 550 °C for 5 h.

The same procedure was applied for the synthesis of ZSM-5 type chromosilicate, only chromium (III) nitrate nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3·9H2O) was used instead of cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate. The pH of the mixture was adjusted to 9.5 and the sample was designed as Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5.

Direct method. In this method, appropriate amounts of CA (corresponding to the GQDs: metalosilicate weight ratio of 1:1 and 1:2) were mixed with the calcined metalosilicate zeolite and dispersed in water. The suspension was sonicated for 2 h and then the pyrolysis of CA was carried out according to the procedure used for the synthesis of GQDs to achieve the GQDs/metalosilicate composites. The composites prepared by this method were designed as d-GQDs/Na[M]ZSM-5x(y:z) which × presents the pH of the metalosilicate synthesis and y:z stands for the GQDs:metalosilicate weight ratio. The letter M in Na[M]ZSM-5 shows the type of metal (Co or Cr) incorporated in the zeolite framework.

Solid State Dispersion (SSD). For preparation of GQDs/metalosilicate composites by SSD method, the previously synthesized GQDs and metalosilicates were mixed together in a desired weight ratio and dispersed in 3 mL of ethanol. The mixture was sonicated for 3 h to obtain a homogeneous suspension and then stirred on a magnetic stirrer for 24 h in a capped vessel. Then the lid was removed and the ethanol was allowed to evaporate under ambient condition. Finally, the composites were dried at 100 °C overnight. The composites prepared by this method were designed as S-GQDs/Na[M]ZSM-5x(y:z). Similar to the direct method, x and y:z present the pH of the metalosilicate synthesis and the GQDs:metalosilicate weight ratio, respectively.

The XRD patterns of samples were collected by an X-ray diffractometer (PHILIPS PW1730) utilizing Cu Kα (k = 1.54 Å) radiation source in the range of 2Θ = 5–50°. Infrared spectra were provided on a Bruker Tensor 27 Fourier transform spectrometer using KBr pellets in the region of 4000–400 cm−1. UV–vis spectra of composites were recorded using a Scinco S4100 spectrophotometer at room temperature in the wavelength range of 300–1000 nm. The morphology of samples was studied using a scanning electron microscope (TESCAN MIRA3) operating at 15 kV equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) facility for the elemental analysis. EDX analysis was performed at 20 kV. BET surface area of samples was obtained from N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms using a Belsorp mini instrument at liquid nitrogen temperature (77 K).

Methylene blue was used as a model dye for the evaluation of the photocatalytic activity of the prepared samples. In this regard, 0.05 g of each catalyst (metalosilicate, GQDs or GQDs/metalosilicate composite) was added to the 50 mL of MB solution with a concentration of 20 ppm. The suspensions were stirred using a magnetic stirrer in darkness for 30 min to establish an equilibrium between the adsorption/desorption processes. Afterwards, the suspensions were irradiated with a light source (a 30 W UV lamp as UV source and a cool white fluorescent lamp as visible light source) and the samples were withdrawn at specific time intervals. The samples were centrifuged and the supernatant was separated to estimate the concentration of MB. The concentration of MB was determined by measurement of the sample absorbance at λmax of MB (668 nm) using a Shimadzu MPC-2200 UV spectrophotometer. The removal efficiency of MB was calculated using Eq. (1):

where C0 and Ct are the initial concentration of MB and its concentration at time t, respectively.

To study the electrochemical activity of samples, electrochemical experiments were performed in a three-electrode configuration with a standard reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE; HydroFlex) and Pt spring as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. An AutoLab Potentiostat Galvanostat instrument was used for all experiments. The electrochemical tests were implemented under 1 M KOH basic conditions at room temperature. For the working electrode, a polished glassy carbon (GC) electrode with a diameter of 3 mm was used. The catalysts ink was prepared using 1 mg of each catalyst in a solution mixture of pure ethanol and deionized (DI) water (4:1) along with 5 μL of 5 % wt. Nafion. The ink was ultrasonically mixed for 1 h. Then, 10 μL of the ink was pipetted out on the polished GC surface. The amount of catalyst for each test was kept at about 0.25 mg cm−2. Finally, the GC electrodes were dried at 80 °C for 90 min under air atmosphere. The same procedure was followed to fabricate the Pt/C (10 %) and RuO2 electrodes.

For analyzing OER and HER processes, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were performed at a scan rate of 0.005 V s−1 from 1.2 to 1.8 V and 0 to −1 V for OER and HER, individually. A current density of 10 mA cm−2 was considered as the criterion to estimate the catalytic activity for both OER and HER. Finally, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out within 100 kHz and 100 mHz with an amplitude of 10 mV.

XRD analysis was used to reveal the structure and crystallinity of GQDs/metalosilicate composites. The XRD patterns of composites synthesized by different methods are shown in Fig. 1 , whereas the XRD patterns of metalosilicates are presented in Fig. S1. As discussed in our previous work [34], the XRD patterns of Na[Co]ZSM-59.5 and Na[Co]ZSM-510.5 show the characteristic peaks of MFI structure. The XRD pattern of Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5 also shows the same peaks indicating the formation of MFI structure. No additional peaks can be detected in the XRD pattern of chromosilicate due to the presence of CrO3 or other chromium oxides. The XRD pattern of Na[Cr[ZSM-5 zeolite which was synthesized in our laboratory previously [35] showed a peak at 31.68° assignable to the crystalline CrO3 phase. The peak was disappeared after washing the sample with 1 N acetic acid or upon calcination under nitrogen flow. Since, the aforesaid peak is not present in the XRD pattern of Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5 sample synthesized in this work, it can be concluded that the method described here gives more pure chromosilicate zeolites. The Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5 preserves the orthorhombic symmetry after calcination which is a good evidence for the incorporation of heteroatom into the zeolite framework in accordance with the literature [35,36].

The XRD patterns of GQDs/metalosilicate composites (Fig. 1a and b) are similar to that of pure metalosilicates and show peaks at 2θ ≈ 7.96, 8.86, 23.19, 23.90, and 24.40° due to the reflection from (011), (200), (501), (303), and (133) planes of metalosilicates with orthorhombic symmetry, respectively. The mean crystallite size of composites was calculated using the Scherrer equation which is about 50.9, 22.3, 69.5, 31.9, 20.5, and 25.3 for d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1), d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1), d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1), S-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1), d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:2), and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1), separately. A broad background appears in the XRD patterns of composites around 2θ = 22–26° that can be attributed to the reflection from (002) plane of GQDs with hexagonal phase. This observation confirms the presence of GQDs in the composites. The broadening of the (002) diffraction peak of GQDs in the composites can be arisen from the low crystallinity of GQDs and also their small size [37].

It is worth noting that the intensity of the metalosilicates characteristic diffraction peaks is weakened in the composites. The intensity of peaks is affected by the content of GQDs in the composites. As can be distinguished from the XRD patterns of S-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:2) and S-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composites, the higher the content of GQDs in the composites the lower the intensity of the metalosilicate peaks.

The presence of GQDs in the composites was established by identifying the bands assignable to the GQDs functional groups in the FT-IR spectra. The FT-IR spectra of Na[Co]ZSM-59.5, GQDs and their corresponding composites are presented in Fig. 2 . The FT-IR spectra of GQDs/ Na[Co]ZSM-510.5 and GQDs/ Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5 composites are shown in Fig. S2. The characteristic bands of GQDs are as following: 841 cm−1 (the bending vibration of the C H bond), 1075 cm−1 (C─OH stretching vibration), 1393 cm−1 (the symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylic groups) and 1572 cm−1 (aromatic CC vibration) [38–42]. Furthermore, a band appears as a shoulder around 1718 cm−1 which can be attributed to the CO stretching vibration of the carboxylic groups [38,43,44]. The characteristic bands of GQDs at 1393 and 1572 cm−1 can be clearly observed in the FT-IR spectra of GQDs/metalosilicates composites prepared by both methods (Fig. 2 and S2) along with the bands related to the vibrational modes of MFI-type zeolites which confirms the successful synthesis of composites. The band located at 1718 cm−1 can also be detected in the FT-IR spectra of composites prepared by direct method. The intensity of GQDs vibrational bands is lower for the composites prepared by direct method especially for d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite. The aromatic CC vibrational band of GQDs slightly shifted to the higher wavenumbers in the FT-IR spectra of composites prepared by SSD method. Whereas, for composites prepared by direct method both bands assigned to the symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylic groups (1393 cm−1) and aromatic CC vibration (1572 cm−1) of GQDs remarkably shifted to the higher wavenumbers. The observed shifts can be resulted from the interaction of functional groups of GQDs with zeolite framework as reported for other GQDs composites [45]. These interactions might reduce the conjugation in GQDs structure and thus increase the CC and CO double bond strength. The larger shifts in FT-IR spectra of composites prepared by direct method reveal the existence of stronger interactions between the GQDs and metalosilicates in these composites. A broad band centered around 3427 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of O H bonds is observed in the FT-IR spectra of all samples. The intensity of this band decreases in the FT-IR spectra of composites prepared by direct method to some extent (Fig. 2b and c) which shows the OH groups are involved in the interaction between components of composites [46]. Whereas the band intensity increases in the FT-IR spectra of composites prepared by SSD method (Fig. 2e and S2 (b)). This can be resulted from the low content of GQDs in these composites, as will be discussed later and their less effective interaction with zeolite.

It is worth noting that the intensity of GQDs bands is not affected significantly by the changing of GQDs:metalosilicate ratio in the direct method. However, for composites prepared by SSD method the intensity of these bands increases by increasing the GQDs:metalosilicate ratio from 1:2 to 1:1.

The morphology of prepared composites was studied using the scanning electron microscope and the provided images are shown in Fig. 3 . SEM image of pure Na[Co]ZSM-59.5 (Fig. 3a) presents typical coffin-shaped morphology of MFI-type zeolites with smooth surface. By introduction of GQDs in d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite, the surface of zeolite particles become relatively rough and GQDs can be seen as bright dots on its surface. The GQDs have spherical morphology with an average size of ∼ 10 nm (inset in Fig. 3b) and are uniformly dispersed on the surface of zeolite. The morphology of composites remains intact after decreasing the GQDs content or changing the type of cobaltosilicate in d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:2) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) composites, respectively (Fig. 3c and d). Some aggregated GQDs are observed in the SEM images of these composites. It is obvious from the SEM images that the content of GQDs in d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) is higher than d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite.

SEM image of Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5 (Fig S3) reveals some small particles on the surface of coffin-shaped zeolite particles which can be attributed to the chromium oxide species. By the synthesis of GQDs in the surface of Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5 (GQDs/chromosilicate composite) the number of surface particles increases (Fig. 3e). The morphology of d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) is similar to the other prepared composites, however; the GQDs are more uniformly dispersed on the surface of the chromosilicate zeolite.

Fig. 3f shows the SEM image of S-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite prepared by SSD method. Although a fraction of GQDs are still present as small individual particles on the surface of zeolite; however, the large number of quantum dots adjoin together and form a flat layer between and/or on the surface of zeolite particles. SEM images reveal that the preparation method can influence the morphology of composites.

The presence of the GQDs in composites was investigated using the EDX analysis. The EDX spectra of composites show peaks corresponding to the C, O, Si and M (Cr or Co) elements (Figs. 3g and S3). The existence of C element confirms the formation of GQDs in the composites. As can be distinguished from EDX analysis, the C content in d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) is higher than d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite which shows the presence of more GQDs in the d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) composite. The Na[Co]ZSM-510.5 zeolite was synthesized at higher pH value than Na[Co]ZSM-59.5 and thus it is anticipated to have more deprotonated silanol groups. The presence of more Si─O─ groups favors the interaction between the functional groups of prepared GQDs and zeolite surface thus enhances the amount of GQDs in the resulting composite. Comparison of C content in GQDs/cobaltosilicate composites and GQDs/chromosilicate (d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1)) composite shows that the d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) has lower GQDs content than GQDs/cobaltosilicate composites. As mentioned above, this composite showed weak bands for the vibrational modes of GQDs functional groups which could be attributed to the low content of GQDs. The S-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite has also lower C content in comparison to the corresponding composite prepared by the direct method.

To investigate the textural properties of GQDs/metalosilicate composites, the N2 adsorption–desorption studies were performed and the results are summarized in Table 1 . Although the BET surface area of metalosilicates are comparable, a higher BET surface area is obtained for Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5. The specific surface area of this sample is also higher than the chromosilicates previously synthesized in our laboratory [35] which can be attributed to the lower amount of extra-framework chromium oxide species. This is consistent with the results of XRD analysis (Fig. S1). By introduction of GQDs in composites, the BET surface area of all samples decreases. The decrement in the surface area is more remarkable for the composites prepared by the direct method. In direct method, the synthesis of GQDs takes place in the presence of metalosilicate; so, the precursor molecules have more opportunity to enter into the channels of zeolite structure. This leads to the formation of GQDs in the pores and channels of metalosilicate framework that hinders the penetration of N2 molecules into the pore structure. But, in SSD method the previously synthesized GQDs are mixed with the metalosilicates and thus cannot pass into the metalosilicate pores as efficiently as composites prepared by the direct method. The blocking of pores by GQDs is also evident from the reduction of total pore and micropore volumes. As can be seen in Table 1, the total pore volume and micropore volume of all composites are decreased in comparison to the pure metalosilicates. Accordingly, for composites prepared by the direct method the pore volume decreases more significantly.

The optical properties of GQDs/metalosilicate composites were studied using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs and GQDs/metalosilicate composites prepared by direct method are presented in Fig. 4 a. The UV–vis spectrum of d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) exhibits a distinct absorption peak at ∼ 351 nm with a shoulder at ∼ 372 nm. The peak located at 351 nm can be ascribed to the n─π* transition of CO bond in GQDs [16,40] which confirms the presence of GQDs in this composite. This peak is also observable in the absorption spectrum of pure GQDs at ∼ 355 nm. The UV–vis spectrum of d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite shows the absorption peak along with the mentioned shoulder in the same regions observed for d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1). Whereas small shifts can be detected in the UV–vis spectrum of d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) composite. The absorption peak appears at 344 nm with a shoulder at 375 nm.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were applied for calculating the band gap energy of composites using the Kubelka-Munk function which is defined as:
(2)
( α h υ )
1 γ
= B
h υ - E g
where α, ʋ, B, and Eg refers to the absorption coefficient, light frequency, the proportionality constant, and band gap energy, respectively. The γ value determines the characteristics of the transition type in a semiconductor that is equal to ½ and 2 for direct and indirectband gap, respectively [47,48]. The band gap energies are obtained by plotting (αhʋ)2 versus hʋ (Tauc’s plot) and extrapolating the linear part of thecurve (Fig. 4b). The band gap energy of GQDs is equal to 3.1 eV which reduces to 3 eV for almost all composites presented in Fig. 4.

The photocatalytic activities of GQDs/metalosilicate composites were evaluated by the degradation of MB under both UV and vis light irradiation. The removal of MB using GQDs/metalosilicate composites takes place through two successive processes, adsorption on composite and photocatalytic degradation. So, prior to the photocatalytic experiments, the adsorption of MB by samples was investigated in the darkness. The adsorption efficiencies by Na[Co]ZSM-59.5, Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5 and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) are depicted in Fig. 5 . As can be seen, the adsorption of MB on all samples reaches to a maximum value at first 30 min and remains almost constant up to 180 min. It can be concluded that the adsorption/desorption equilibrium is achieved at first 30 min. Hence all samples were exposed to MB solution for 30 min in darkness before illumination by the light. It should be mentioned that the adsorbed amount of MB by d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) is higher than that of pure cobaltosilicate. The adsorption efficiency is about 44 % and 66 % for Na[Co]ZSM-59.5 and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1), respectively. Although the synthesis of GQDs on the surface and channels of cobaltosilicate leads to the decrement of BET surface area, but the presence of GQDs with functional groups causes the higher adsorption of dye on the composite. The Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5 zeolite could adsorb about 39 % of MB after 180 min.

To determine the adsorption isotherm model of GQDs/metalosilicate composites, d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) was chosen as adsorbent and the adsorption experiments were conducted with the initial concentration of MB in the range of 10 ppm to 30 ppm for 180 min. The obtained data then was modelled opting Langmuir (3, 4) and Freundlich (5) adsorption isotherms:

where qe
(mg/g) is the amount of methylene blue adsorbed per unit weight of adsorbent at equilibrium; Ce
(mg/L) is the equilibrium concentration of methylene blue; qm
(mg/g) is the maximum amount of methylene blue per unit weight of adsorbent for complete monolayer coverage, and KL
(L/mg) is the Langmuir isotherm constant which quantitatively represents the adsorbent–adsorbate affinity and is related to the energy of the adsorption. Generally, high qm and low values of KL are needed on an excellent adsorbent. RL
is a dimensionless factor named as separation factor, which is calculated through other obtained factors and initial adsorbate concentration (C0 ). Regarding the Freundlich adsorption equation KF

n ) is the Freundlich constant that represents the relative adsorption capacity, higher values of KF demonstrate greater adsorption capacity.

is a dimensionless factor that shows the strength of the adsorption process and the distribution of active sites.

ranging between 0 and 1 is also an amount of the strength of adsorption or surface heterogeneity which becomes more heterogeneous when closer to zero. It has been proven that n values between 1 and 10 represent a beneficial adsorption process [49,50]. The obtained results for d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite are provided in Table 2 .

It is observed that the adsorbent does not perform any compatibility to Freundlich adsorption model, while the compatibility to Langmuir model is not high but acceptable. The maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbent was calculated as 88.4956 mg g−1. According to the RL value, which is between 0 and 1, it is deduced that the reaction is favorable. However, KL that represents the strength of binding was found to be low for the adsorbent. The Freundlich constants were calculated, but they were neglected due to the very low regression factor.

The results of photocatalytic degradation of MB by GQDs and GQDs/metalosilicate composites under UV light irradiation are presented in Fig. 6 , where the removal efficiencies are plotted against the degradation time. As can be seen, for pure GQDs the removal efficiency increases at first 60 min and remains almost constant up to 210 min of photocatalytic reaction. For GQDs/metalosilicate composites the photocatalytic degradation efficiency greatly depends on the composite’s preparation method. It is obvious that composites prepared by SSD method have lower removal efficiencies and their higher BET surface area cannot improve their removal efficiency. This can originate from the low content of GQDs in these composites. As discussed before (section 3.2.1) the presence of GQDs, with various functional groups increases the efficiency of dye adsorption by composites. On the other hand, GQDs are responsible for the photocatalytic degradation of MB under light irradiation but according to the SEM images, the GQDs are less uniformly dispersed on the surface of zeolite particles in these composites which leads to the lower adsorption of light and lower photocatalytic activity. It is worth noting that the removal efficiency decreases after first 30 min (adsorption in darkness) for S-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:2) and S-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composites (Fig. 6g and i). This phenomenon can be attributed to the desorption of MB under light irradiation. In other words, the desorption process exceeds the adsorption/photocatalytic degradation in these composites due to the low content of GQDs that leads to the decrease in total removal efficiency. For other SSD composites the removal efficiency gradually increases under UV light till 210 min.

Comparison of photocatalytic activities of different composites prepared by direct method reveals that the removal efficiency is the highest for d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite. In fact, d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) can adsorb about 96 % of MB during first 30 min (in darkness) and then the degradation of adsorbed MB takes place during irradiation by light, so the removal efficiency remains almost constant after first 30 min. This higher removal efficiency can be attributed to the uniform dispersion of GQDs in this composite but not to the higher content of GQDs. It was demonstrated using EDX analysis that the d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite has lower content of GQDs in comparison to the GQDs/cobaltosilicate composites. For GQDs/cobaltosilicate composites, the removal efficiency is also affected by the synthesis condition of cobaltosilicate substrate. The removal efficiency of MB by d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) is 69 and 88 %, respectively. The difference can be attributed to the presence of more GQDs in the d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) composite as confirmed by SEM images and EDX analysis. On the other hand, decreasing the GQDs content in the d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:2) composite leads to the decreasing of the removal efficiency by 17 % when compared to d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) composite. The lower the GQDs content the lower the adsorption of light by the photocatalyst. The lower adsorption of light leads to the lower photocatalytic activity and hence the lower removal efficiency.

The photocatalytic activity of GQDs/metalosilicate composites with GQDs:metalosilicate ratio of 1:1 was tested under visible light irradiation and the results are provided in Fig. 7 . As can be seen, the photocatalytic activity of d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) under visible light increases and the removal efficiency reaches to 88 % after 210 min. The removal efficiency for d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) does not change significantly after changing the light source, whereas for d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) the removal efficiency decreases by 9 % under visible light.

The total degradation efficiencies of all samples under UV and vis light are compared in Fig. 8 . As can be seen the composites prepared by direct method are capable photocatalysts for the degradation of MB under both UV and visible light. These composites are promising photocatalysts for the degradation of dye under sunlight that eliminates the need for any light source.

Both pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were examined for investigation of the removal kinetic by composites possessing the higher removal efficiencies. The results revealed that all the selected samples follow the pseudo-second-order kinetic model as shown in Fig. 9 . The pseudo-second-order kinetic model is expressed as:

C0 and Ct (mg/L) are the initial concentration of MB and its concentration at time t, and V and W are the volume of solvent (mL) and weight of photocatalyst (mg), respectively [51]. The rate constants calculated using the intercepts of plots are also shown in Fig. 9 for all samples. The good linear plots with the high correlation coefficients for all the studied samples suggest that the photodegradation of MB by these composites follows pseudo-second-order kinetic model under both UV and vis light. On the other hand, the rate of photocatalytic removal of MB using composites is affected by the type of metalosilicate present in the composite and the type of light source. The highest rate constant is obtained for the removal of MB by d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) under UV light among the tested composites.

The photocatalytic performance of GQDs:metalosilicate composites involves the photoexcitation of valance band electrons of GQDs to the conduction bond under light irradiation. The photoinduced electrons and holes can participate in the subsequent reactions with water and O2 molecules and produce reactive oxygen species. Fan et al. have investigated the nature of these reactive oxygen species using ESR spin-trip technique. The results confirmed that OH● and ●●O─2 radicals are generated when the GQDs are illuminated by visible light. They concluded these radicals are responsible for the degradation of organic compounds such as methylene blue [16]. Kumar et al. claimed that the photoinduced holes in the valence band of GQDs react with the pollutants directly. They cannot produce OH● from OH─ and H2O, because the reduction potential of OH─/OH● and H2O/OH● is more positive than the valance band of GQDs [37]. Both OH● and photogenerated holes are strong oxidizing agents for the degradation of various organic pollutants.

It is worth to note that the presence of metalosilicates as substrate for GQDs can enhance the electron-hole pair separation. The photogenerated electrons can be transferred to the surface of zeolite and trapped in the surface defects. So, the electron and hole are separated effectively and thus the life time of photogenerated species is increased [23].

The Oxygen Evolution Reaction (OER) is considered as an index for the anodic half-reaction in the water splitting process. The real OER mechanism is complicated due to the four-electron transfer pathway of the OER process [52,53]. These reactions are usually assumed as follows:
OH– + M → MOH + e– (8)
MOH + OH– → MO + H2O + e– (9)
MO + OH– → MOOH + e– (10)
MOOH + OH– → M + O2 + H2O + e– (11)

Therefore, the final water oxidation reaction under alkaline conditions is:
4OH– → O2 (g) + 2H2O (l) + 4e– (12)

The OER catalytic activity of best-performing photocatalysts, discussed above, was assessed on the GC electrode in 1 mol/L KOH solution. The related LSV polarization curves are portrayed in Fig. 10 a. The polarization curve of GC (as a neutral substrate), commercial RuO2 (as the reference material for OER), pure GQDs and Na[Co]ZSM-59.5 zeolite were also measured for comparison. Observation of polarization curves indicates that the GC electrode has a negligible current density within the same potential window. Besides, RuO2 reveals potential of 1.52 V versus their equilibrium value of 1.23 V (vs RHE) at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and thus an overpotential of 290 mV, suggesting a high overpotential for the OER process. In the sense of GQDs/metalosilicate electrocatalysts, only d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1), d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:2) catalysts reached a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and demonstrated a reasonable overpotential for the OER process. Based on the results, d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1), d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:2) catalysts demand the respective potentials of 1.68, 1.70 and 1.72 V(vs RHE) (thus overpotentials of 0.45, 0.47 and 0.49 V) to reach a current density of 10 mA cm−2.

To determine the OER reaction kinetics of the as-obtained materials, the Tafel slopes of all samples were calculated, and the results were depicted in Fig. 10b. The Tafel slope of d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) sample (90.7 mV dec−1) is the lowest value among the three samples and close to the RuO2, as reference material, indicating a faster growth of current density (facile reaction kinetics) as a function of overpotential. Therefore, the greater OER activity of GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) relative to other samples stems from the more facile kinetics of this catalyst.

Analogous to OER, the performance of the GQDs/metalosilicate samples in terms of the Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER) was evaluated on the GC electrode in 1 mol/L KOH solution. The relevant LSV curves can be visualized in Fig. 10c. For comparison, the result of Pt/C (10 %) as a state-of-the-art HER electrocatalyst has been introduced into the graphs with an overpotential of 198 mV to deliver 10 mA cm−2. For HER activity, only d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) catalysts reached the yardstick of 10 mA cm−2 and displayed overpotentials of 0.59 and 0.57 V for the HER process, separately. d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) requires less HER overpotential as opposed to the d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) to afford 10 mA cm−2.

For the case of HER, Tafel slope is a fundamental parameter to appraise the rate-determining step through the HER and in turn ascertain the HER reaction kinetics. For this, the related Tafel plots are shown in Fig. 10d, exhibiting a direct relationship between current density and overpotential. With reference to earlier reports [54] two general routes can be envisaged for the evolution of hydrogen (adsorption and desorption) under alkaline media, either it should conform to Volmer (adsorption step), Heyrovsky mechanism (Equations 13 and 14) or the Volmer-Tafel mechanism (Eqs. 15 and 16):
Volmer step: H2O + e− → OH– + Had (13)
Heyrovsky step: H2O + e− + Had → OH– + H2 (14)
Volmer step: H2O + e− → OH– + Had (15)
Tafel step: Had + Had → H2 (16)

where Had is the adsorbed hydrogen on the surface of the catalyst under basic solutions. Within alkaline conditions, hydrogen binds to the catalyst via the Volmer adsorption, followed by the desorption process through the Heyrovsky step (so-called electrochemical desorption step) or Tafel step (known as chemical desorption step). The estimated values in terms of Tafel slope for the Volmer, Heyrovsky, and Tafel steps are as follows ∼120 mV dec−1, ∼40 mV dec−1, and ∼30 mV dec−1, respectively [55,56]. Concerning the Tafel plots featured in Fig. 10d, it can be claimed that d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) have similar Tafel slope values (66.2 and 67.4 mV dec−1, respectively) even smaller than that of commercial Pt/C catalyst (85.2 mV dec−1). To this end, the Tafel slopes of these materials fall within the range of 40–120 mV dec−1, implying that the adsorption takes place by Volmer step and desorption by Heyrovsky step.

The EIS experiments were performed to examine the charge transfer resistance on the surface of the catalysts once they are in contact with the solution. Fig. 11 depicts the relevant Nyquist plots and corresponding equivalent circuits. In the schematic circuit, Re, CPE, Rct, and W stand for the respective resistance of the solution between the working and reference electrodes, the interfacial capacitive component, Faraday resistance, and Warburg impedance to express the diffusion controlling step on the surface.

According to the results obtained from fitting data by ZView software (Table 3 ), it is evident that d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) catalysts enjoy low Rct and W resistances, while other samples suffer from high resistances. For these catalysts, the low Rct, smaller Warburg impedance along with relatively large capacitance (about 10−5 mFsp−1) showcase their high conductive surfaces in contrast to other samples, confirming the superiority of the former to the latter in terms of electrocatalysis.

In this work, GQDs/ZSM-5 type cobaltosilicate and GQDs/ZSM-5 type chromosilicate composites with different GQDs:metalosilicate ratios were synthesized using direct and SSD methods. The obtained composites were thoroughly characterized using XRD, FT-IR, SEM, UV–vis absorption, and N2 adsorption–desorption analysis. The XRD patterns revealed the synthesis of pure metalosilicates which the intensity of their characteristic diffraction peaks was weakened upon preparation of composites. The presence of GQDs in the composites was established by the appearance of a broad background around 22–25° in the XRD patterns. FT-IR analysis brought more evidence for the synthesis of GQDs/metalosilicate composites. The bands assignable to the symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylic groups and aromatic CC vibration of GQDs were observed in the FT-IR spectra of all composites. SEM images revealed the dispersion of GQDs in the composites is affected by the synthesis method. A more uniform dispersion of GQDs was obtained by synthesis of composites using directed method. The decrement of BET surface area also was remarkable for composites prepared by direct method which was attributed to the formation of GQDs in the pores and channels of metalosilicates. The blocking of pores by GQDs hinders the penetration of N2 molecules into the pore system. The prepared composites were tested for the photodegradation of methylene blue under both UV and vis light. The results confirmed that the composites prepared by direct method are efficient photocatalysts for the degradation of MB. The removal efficiencies for these composites were higher than the composites prepared by SSD method and also the pure GQDs. The best photocatalytic activity under UV light was observed for d-GQDs/Na[Cr]ZSM-59.5(1:1) followed by d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1). The higher photocatalytic activity of these composites was attributed to the uniform dispersion of GQDs which leads to the efficient absorption of light. The removal efficiency decreased by decreasing the content of GQDs in composites. The composites prepared by direct method were used as electrocatalysts for water splitting. Both d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) composites showed better electrocatalytic activity for water splitting in comparison to pure GQDs and metalosilicates. The observed overpotentials for the HER at current density of 10 mA cm−2 were about 0.59 and 0.57 V for d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1), respectively. Whereas the OER required an overpotential of 0.45 and 0.47 V in the presence of GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-59.5(1:1) and d-GQDs/Na[Co]ZSM-510.5(1:1) to reach the current density of 10 mA cm−2. The low Rct and smaller Warburg impedance obtained for these catalysts reveal their high conductive surfaces in comparison to the pure constituents.
